text:
CT SCAN OF THE CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
INDICATION / CLINICAL HISTORY: 58-year-old male with newly diagnosed sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma on colonoscopic biopsy. Concurrent hepatic lesions identified incidentally on outside-facility ultrasound; hepatic segment VII lesion core-biopsy confirmed metastatic adenocarcinoma. Study requested for oncologic staging prior to initiation of systemic therapy.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed in the portal venous phase following administration of 100 mL of iohexol-based intravenous contrast at a rate of 3 mL/sec. Oral contrast (dilute gastrografin) administered 60 minutes prior to scanning. Reconstructions in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes; 3 mm slice thickness. No known contrast allergies; creatinine 0.98 mg/dL reviewed prior to administration.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:
Lungs and airways: The lungs are clear bilaterally. No pulmonary nodules, masses, or areas of consolidation are identified. The airways are patent to the level of the subsegmental bronchi. No endobronchial lesions. No ground-glass opacities or interstitial thickening.
Pleura: No pleural effusions. No pneumothorax. No pleural thickening or nodularity to suggest pleural disease.
Mediastinum and hila: The mediastinum is midline. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes. The thymus is not prominent. No pericardial effusion. The visualized cardiac chambers are normal in size. The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber without evidence of aneurysm or dissection.
Chest wall and bones: No rib lesions or sternal lesions to suggest osseous metastatic disease. No pathologic fractures identified. No soft tissue chest wall masses.

ABDOMEN -- LIVER:
The liver is mildly enlarged, measuring approximately 18.7 cm in the craniocaudal dimension. The hepatic parenchyma demonstrates heterogeneous attenuation with multiple discrete hypodense lesions scattered throughout both lobes, none of which demonstrate enhancement in the portal venous phase and all of which are conspicuous against the enhancing background parenchyma. Specifically:
- Hepatic segment II (left lobe, lateral superior subsegment): a rounded, well-circumscribed hypodense lesion measuring approximately 1.4 cm in greatest axial dimension. The lesion demonstrates minimal peripheral rim-like hypoenhancement and a central low-density core, morphologically consistent with a viable hepatic metastatic deposit.
- Hepatic segment IVa (left lobe, medial superior subsegment): a lobulated hypodense lesion measuring approximately 2.1 cm in greatest axial dimension. Peripheral infiltrative margin is noted along the superior aspect. No central necrosis or hemorrhage.
- Hepatic segment VI (right lobe, posterior inferior subsegment): a rounded hypodense lesion measuring approximately 1.7 cm in greatest axial dimension. The lesion abuts the inferior right hepatic vein without evidence of frank vascular encasement or intraluminal tumor thrombus on this study.
- Hepatic segment VII (right lobe, posterior superior subsegment): the dominant hepatic lesion, measuring approximately 3.2 cm in greatest axial dimension. The lesion is heterogeneously hypodense with a lobulated contour and demonstrates faint peripheral enhancement. It lies in close proximity to the right hepatic vein and the right anterior sectoral pedicle; no definitive vascular invasion is seen, but the spatial relationship is notable.
No additional satellite lesions are identified on this study. The intrahepatic biliary ducts are not dilated. No biliary obstruction. The portal vein and hepatic veins are patent without filling defects or evidence of tumor thrombus. No hepatic abscess. The gallbladder is present and unremarkable without stones, wall thickening, or pericholecystic fluid.

ABDOMEN -- REMAINING UPPER ABDOMINAL ORGANS:
Spleen: Normal in size and attenuation (9.2 cm craniocaudal). No splenic lesions.
Pancreas: Unremarkable throughout its entire length including the head, uncinate, body, and tail. No pancreatic ductal dilatation. No peripancreatic fat stranding or fluid.
Adrenal glands: Bilaterally small and morphologically normal. No adrenal nodules or masses. No adrenal thickening.
Kidneys: Both kidneys are normal in size with maintained corticomedullary differentiation and symmetric enhancement. No hydronephrosis. A 4 mm simple-appearing cortical cyst is noted in the upper pole of the right kidney, too small to fully characterize but statistically benign. No renal masses.
Aorta and great vessels: The abdominal aorta is normal in caliber. The celiac axis, superior mesenteric artery, and inferior mesenteric artery are patent. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy along the aortocaval, para-aortic, or mesenteric vessel chains.

ABDOMEN AND PELVIS -- COLORECTUM AND MESENTERY:
Sigmoid colon: A circumferential, heterogeneously enhancing mural mass is identified in the mid-to-distal sigmoid colon, measuring approximately 4.8 cm in greatest axial dimension. The mass demonstrates irregular luminal narrowing without evidence of complete high-grade obstruction on this study; the proximal colon is non-obstructed and non-dilated. There is moderate pericolic fat stranding extending into the pericolonic mesentery consistent with tumor extension beyond the muscularis propria into the pericolorectal tissues. The fat planes between the mass and adjacent pelvic sidewall structures are preserved. No definitive involvement of the bladder wall, posterior vaginal wall equivalent structures, seminal vesicles, or pelvic floor musculature is appreciated. The ureters are not displaced or obstructed.
Lymph nodes -- mesentery and pelvis: Multiple enlarged mesenteric lymph nodes are identified along the sigmoidal and left colic vessels. The largest discrete node measures approximately 1.9 cm in short-axis dimension, with rounded morphology and loss of fatty hilum, morphologically worrisome for nodal metastatic involvement. Several additional subcentimeter nodes with similar round morphology are noted in the same mesenteric distribution. No enlarged pelvic sidewall, iliac chain, or inguinal lymph nodes.
Remainder of colon: The remainder of the colon, including the cecum, ascending, transverse, descending, and rectum, appears unremarkable. No additional colonic masses or obstructing lesions. No diverticulitis. A 3 cm sessile appearing lesion is incidentally noted immediately adjacent to the primary sigmoid tumor, likely representing a polyp; this region is predominantly obscured by the primary mass and the degree of independent assessment is limited on CT.

PERITONEAL CAVITY AND RETROPERITONEUM:
No ascites. The peritoneal surfaces appear smooth and unremarkable. No omental nodularity, caking, or peritoneal implants are identified to suggest peritoneal carcinomatosis on this examination. No retroperitoneal masses or lymphadenopathy.

PELVIS:
The urinary bladder is adequately distended and demonstrates a smooth, thin wall without intraluminal masses or filling defects. The prostate gland appears mildly enlarged, measuring approximately 38 cc, consistent with the patient's age; no focal suspicious lesions. The seminal vesicles are symmetric and unremarkable. No pelvic free fluid.

BONES (IMAGED THORACIC AND LUMBAR SPINE, PELVIS, PROXIMAL FEMORA):
No lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions are identified to suggest skeletal metastatic disease. No pathologic compression fractures. Mild multilevel lumbar degenerative disc disease with anterior and posterior osteophyte formation, consistent with age-related changes.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. SIGMOID COLON MASS: Circumferential, infiltrative sigmoid colon mass measuring approximately 4.8 cm, with transmural extension and associated pericolic fat stranding, consistent with the known primary adenocarcinoma. No definitive involvement of adjacent pelvic organs. Moderate regional mesenteric lymphadenopathy (largest node 1.9 cm) with morphologic features worrisome for nodal metastatic involvement.

2. HEPATIC METASTASES: Multiple bilobar hepatic hypodense lesions as described above involving segments II (1.4 cm), IVa (2.1 cm), VI (1.7 cm), and VII (3.2 cm, dominant lesion), consistent with synchronous hepatic metastatic disease. The dominant segment VII lesion lies in close proximity to the right hepatic vein but no definitive vascular invasion is appreciated on this study. The bilobar distribution and the proximity of the segment VII lesion to major hepatic vasculature are noted for surgical planning purposes.

3. NO PULMONARY METASTASES: Lungs clear bilaterally. No pulmonary nodules, masses, or pleural disease.

4. NO PERITONEAL DISEASE: Peritoneal surfaces appear smooth. No ascites or omental nodularity identified to suggest peritoneal carcinomatosis on this examination.

5. NO OSSEOUS METASTASES: No lytic or sclerotic bone lesions on this examination.

6. INCIDENTAL FINDINGS: Mildly enlarged prostate gland (age-appropriate). Tiny simple right renal cortical cyst. Lumbar spondylosis.

Overall, findings are consistent with locally advanced sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with synchronous bilobar hepatic metastatic disease (M1a). Absence of pulmonary, peritoneal, or osseous spread is noted. Clinical and surgical team correlation is recommended for multidisciplinary staging discussion and treatment planning.
